chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: AWS, the world's largest collection of awful web services.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: It was created by Jeff Bezos in 2006 at his secret volcano base as a diabolical plan to

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: get startups addicted to cheap computing resources.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Like any good crack dealer, they start you off with a free taste.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Then pricing scales up exponentially as you become more dependent.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: By the time you realize you're locked in, it's too late.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Your business will die from withdrawals if you try to quit.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Currently, it has exactly 69,420 different services to choose from, each one with its

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: own stupid name and nonsensical icon.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Some are acronyms, some have numbers, some are planets, and some are low-key insults at

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: their rivals, like Redshift.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: It's so complex that you'll want to get certified with a PhD before even attempting to launch

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: a Hello World.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: And be careful, because many of the services are like beta products that haven't been

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: updated in years.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: But impressively, they all still run like clockwork.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: It feels a lot like developing in a haunted amusement park.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: And if they don't have what you're looking for, just call 1-800-AMAZON and they'll launch

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: a brand new service for you the next day, because the customer is always right.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Or, if you're building an awesome open source tool, they will immediately launch a service

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: for it so you can never ever make any money from your hard work.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: To get started, go ahead and sign up for an account.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Then the first thing you'll notice is that your account was immediately hacked, and you

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: now have 500 EC2 instances mining shitcoins all over the world.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Conveniently, there's no big red shutdown button, and what you'll have to do is go and

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: click on each server individually to manually shut it off.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Meanwhile, if you open up the billing service, you'll notice the amount of money you owe has broken

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: the CSS because the number is too large.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: At this point, you have two options.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: You can sign out of the application and pretend this was all just a bad dream.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Or you can contact support and beg for mercy.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: They'll forward your ticket to Jeff's $500 million sailing yacht, where he decides your

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: fate like Emperor Nero.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: If you get a thumbs down, it means you're now homeless, but a thumbs up means we can

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: explore the UI.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Each team at AWS gets to design their own UI, giving us a wonderful, inconsistent freak show

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: of a website.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: You've got some products using Bootstrap One, others that look like they were designed on

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Fiverr, and some that are absolutely beautiful.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Now, if you can't find what you're looking for, don't even try to use the search.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Instead, just go straight to Google or chat GPT.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: In the words of God's chosen programmer, an idiot admires complexity, a genius admires simplicity.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: None of us here are geniuses, and that's why we use AWS.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: This has been me trashing a technology that has me by the balls in 100 seconds.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Big mistake.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: In reality, though, AWS has revolutionized the way we think about IT infrastructure.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: It's the most popular cloud platform for a reason, and without it, the barriers to entry

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: for the average tech startup would be much higher, and for that, it deserves our respect.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Make sure to also check out my full breakdown of AWS.

chunks.title: AWS
chunks.text: Thanks for watching, and I will see you in the next one.

text: AWS, the world's largest collection of awful web services. It was created by Jeff Bezos in 2006 at his secret volcano base as a diabolical plan to get startups addicted to cheap computing resources. Like any good crack dealer, they start you off with a free taste. Then pricing scales up exponentially as you become more dependent. By the time you realize you're locked in, it's too late. Your business will die from withdrawals if you try to quit. Currently, it has exactly 69,420 different services to choose from, each one with its own stupid name and nonsensical icon. Some are acronyms, some have numbers, some are planets, and some are low-key insults at their rivals, like Redshift. It's so complex that you'll want to get certified with a PhD before even attempting to launch a Hello World. And be careful, because many of the services are like beta products that haven't been updated in years. But impressively, they all still run like clockwork. It feels a lot like developing in a haunted amusement park. And if they don't have what you're looking for, just call 1-800-AMAZON and they'll launch a brand new service for you the next day, because the customer is always right. Or, if you're building an awesome open source tool, they will immediately launch a service for it so you can never ever make any money from your hard work. To get started, go ahead and sign up for an account. Then the first thing you'll notice is that your account was immediately hacked, and you now have 500 EC2 instances mining shitcoins all over the world. Conveniently, there's no big red shutdown button, and what you'll have to do is go and click on each server individually to manually shut it off. Meanwhile, if you open up the billing service, you'll notice the amount of money you owe has broken the CSS because the number is too large. At this point, you have two options. You can sign out of the application and pretend this was all just a bad dream. Or you can contact support and beg for mercy. They'll forward your ticket to Jeff's $500 million sailing yacht, where he decides your fate like Emperor Nero. If you get a thumbs down, it means you're now homeless, but a thumbs up means we can explore the UI. Each team at AWS gets to design their own UI, giving us a wonderful, inconsistent freak show of a website. You've got some products using Bootstrap One, others that look like they were designed on Fiverr, and some that are absolutely beautiful. Now, if you can't find what you're looking for, don't even try to use the search. Instead, just go straight to Google or chat GPT. In the words of God's chosen programmer, an idiot admires complexity, a genius admires simplicity. None of us here are geniuses, and that's why we use AWS. This has been me trashing a technology that has me by the balls in 100 seconds. Big mistake. In reality, though, AWS has revolutionized the way we think about IT infrastructure. It's the most popular cloud platform for a reason, and without it, the barriers to entry for the average tech startup would be much higher, and for that, it deserves our respect. Make sure to also check out my full breakdown of AWS. Thanks for watching, and I will see you in the next one.
